# Reproducibility and the Replication Crisis

A result you cannot reproduce is not yet a result. It is a claim. For
most of the history of empirical economics, the distinction did not
bite, because nobody could check: the data sat on the author's hard
drive, the code lived in a folder named `final_v3_REALfinal`, and the
published tables were the only thing the rest of us ever saw. That
era is over. Journals now run your code before they accept your paper,
funders require your data to be archived, and a coauthor, a referee,
or a stranger on the internet can ask to rebuild your headline number
from scratch. This chapter is about why that happened, and why version
control, the subject of the rest of this book, became a basic
research skill rather than a programmer's hobby.

The audience here is economics, finance, and accounting first, and the
wider social sciences alongside. The evidence leads with economics
because that is where this book lives, but the problem is not an
economics problem. It is a science problem, and seeing its full width
is part of taking it seriously.

## Two words people use interchangeably and should not

It helps to separate two things that casual usage blurs together.

**Reproducibility** is the narrow, mechanical question: given *your*
data and *your* code, can someone else (or you, in two years) run them
and get *your* published numbers? This is a property of your files and
your computer, not of the world. If it fails, something is broken:
a missing data step, an unstated software version, a hard-coded path,
a lost random seed.

**Replicability** is the larger scientific question: if someone
gathers *new* data and runs a *comparable* analysis, do they reach a
consistent conclusion? This is a property of the finding, not the
files. It can fail even when your code is perfect, because the
original effect was noise, was specific to one sample, or was inflated
by selective reporting.

The terms get used in opposite ways across fields, so always check
which one a given paper means. This book can only directly help with
the first: Git and a disciplined project layout make your work
*reproducible*. But reproducibility is the precondition for everything
else. A finding that cannot even be reproduced from its own files
cannot be defended, corrected, or built on, and the replication
evidence below suggests that bar is missed far more often than anyone
is comfortable with.

## The economics evidence

Start close to home. In 2015, Andrew Chang and Phillip Li, then at the
Federal Reserve Board, tried to reproduce 67 empirical papers from 13
well-regarded economics journals, using the authors' own data and
code. Not to test whether the findings were *true*, just whether the
published numbers came back out of the published materials. Working
from the replication files alone, without contacting the authors, they
reproduced the key qualitative result of 22 of 67 papers, about 33
percent. Even after writing to authors for help, and setting aside
papers using confidential data or software they lacked, they got to 29
of 59, about 49 percent. Their conclusion is quotable precisely
because it is so blunt: "we are able to replicate less than half of
the papers in our sample even with help from the authors," and so "we
assert that economics research is usually not replicable."

One detail in their numbers is the whole argument of this book in
miniature. Among papers whose journals *required* data and code to be
deposited, they obtained working replication files for 83 percent.
Among papers whose journals did *not* require it, they obtained them
for 42 percent. The single biggest predictor of whether the work could
be checked was not the topic or the authors' care. It was whether an
institution forced the materials into the open.

Experimental economics tells the same story from a different angle. In
a 2016 *Science* study, Colin Camerer and a large team pre-registered
and re-ran 18 laboratory experiments published in the *American
Economic Review* and the *Quarterly Journal of Economics* between 2011
and 2014, with at least 90 percent statistical power to detect the
original effects. They found a significant effect in the same
direction as the original for 11 of the 18, about 61 percent. And
where effects did appear, they were smaller than first reported: on
average the replicated effect size was 66 percent of the original.
These were strong studies in the best journals, run by sympathetic
replicators. A third still did not hold up.

## The same crack runs through the social sciences

If this were unique to economics, it might be a disciplinary quirk.
It is not. The most cited single result came from psychology: in 2015
the Open Science Collaboration repeated 100 studies from three leading
psychology journals. In the originals, 97 percent reported a
statistically significant effect. In the replications, 36 percent did.
The replicated effects, where they survived, were about half the
magnitude of the published ones. A field looked at its own flagship
results and could reproduce roughly one in three.

The bridge back to the social sciences generally was built in 2018,
when Camerer's group turned the same machinery on 21 experimental
social-science papers published in *Nature* and *Science* between 2010
and 2015, the most visible venues there are. They confirmed a
significant same-direction effect for 13 of 21, about 62 percent,
again at roughly half the original effect size. The number lands in
the same band as the economics studies, which is the point: across
economics, psychology, and the broad social sciences, somewhere around
a third to a half of published, peer-reviewed, headline findings do
not come back when someone careful tries to get them back.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Notice what these projects could and could not do. They could attempt
*replication* (new data) only after first achieving *reproducibility*
(getting the original analysis to run at all). Chang and Li spent much
of their effort just obtaining files that would execute. Every hour a
replicator spends reconstructing your environment is an hour not spent
checking your science. A clean, version-controlled, rebuild-from-code
project is not bureaucratic compliance. It is the thing that lets your
work enter the scientific record instead of stalling at the door.
:::

## Why this is now your problem, not just a debate

Two responses to this evidence matter for how you will actually work.

The first is cultural: incentives reward novel, significant, publishable
findings and barely reward careful, reproducible ones, which is most of
how the literature filled up with results that do not return. You
cannot fix the incentive system by yourself. You can refuse to add to
the pile.

The second is institutional, and it has teeth. The American Economic
Association, publisher of the *AER* and the *AEJ* family, now operates
a Data and Code Availability Policy enforced by a dedicated Data
Editor. The policy is explicit: "Prior to acceptance, authors of
papers that contain empirical work, simulations, or experimental work
must provide the data, code, and other details of the computations
sufficient to permit replication," deposited in a trusted repository.
And the Association does not take this on trust. The Data Editor "will
assess compliance with this policy, including by conducting
reproducibility checks and verifying the accuracy of the information
provided." In practice that means a member of the editorial staff
downloads your replication package and, within reasonable limits of
time and computing, actually runs your code to confirm it produces the
numbers in your accepted paper, before that paper is published. This
model, an editor who runs your code, has spread across the top
economics journals and into political science and other fields through
a shared Data and Code Availability Standard.

So the question "can a stranger rebuild my results from my files?" is
no longer hypothetical or optional. It is a step between acceptance and
publication at the journals you most want to publish in. The skills in
this book are how you pass it without a scramble. Chapter 15 returns to
the full replication package; here the point is only that
reproducibility has become a gate, and version control is how you walk
through it calmly.

## What Git does, and what it does not

A warning that will shape every chapter that follows. Git, the tool
this book teaches, versions *text*: your code, your scripts, your
notes, your manuscript. It does this superbly. It does *not*, on its
own, reproduce two things that reproducibility equally depends on:
your **data** and your **software environment**. A repository that
faithfully tracks every line of code but assumes a 2 GB dataset nobody
else can obtain, running under package versions nobody recorded, is
version-controlled and still not reproducible. Closing that gap, with
seeded data generators, data repositories and DOIs, and locked
environments, is the subject of Chapter 8. Keep the distinction in
view from the start: Git is necessary for reproducible research, and it
is not sufficient.

## The worked case: this book's own example

Throughout the book we use one running example, a small, fully
simulated minimum-wage difference-in-differences study living in
`sandbox/minwage-did`. It is built to be a model of the practices this
chapter argues for, and we will keep returning to it. Its entire
history is four commits:

```bash
$ git log --oneline
dcb18de Note headline result in README
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
758175e Add polars cleaning script
20c7835 Set up project skeleton and data generator
```

That is the whole project told as a sequence of labelled snapshots:
skeleton and data generator, then cleaning, then analysis, then the
written-up result. Anyone can read how the study was built by reading
its history, and anyone can rebuild it, because the data are produced
by a seeded script rather than shipped as a file, and the software
versions are pinned in a lockfile. Code in Git, data from a seed,
environment in a lockfile: that combination is what makes the example
reproducible, and it is the spine of everything that follows.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The most common way research fails to reproduce is not fraud or even
incompetence. It is drift: an undocumented manual edit to a
spreadsheet, a figure regenerated from data that quietly changed, a
result from a script that no longer exists in the form that produced
it. Every one of these is a thing version control is built to prevent.
The danger is not a dramatic disaster; it is the slow, invisible
divergence between what you published and what your files can now
produce.
:::

## What you can now do

You can state, precisely, why reproducibility matters and how it
differs from replicability; cite the economics and broader
social-science evidence that roughly a third to a half of published
findings do not reproduce; and explain why journals now enforce data
and code availability before they will publish you. What you cannot yet
do is the practical work of making your own projects reproducible. That
starts in the next chapter, with what Git actually is, and continues
through the daily habits in Chapter 5. The argument is made; the rest
of the book is the method.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

No Git commands yet, just an honest audit of your current practice.

1. Take your most recent empirical project. Could a careful stranger,
   given only what you would hand over today, reproduce your main table
   without emailing you? Write down, concretely, every step that would
   break.
2. For that same project, separate the two failure modes from this
   chapter: which problems are *reproducibility* gaps (your own files
   will not produce your own numbers) and which are *replicability*
   questions (the finding itself might not hold in new data)?
3. Look up whether your target journal has a data and code availability
   policy, and whether it verifies submissions. If it does, read what
   it would require of you, then ask whether you could meet it this
   week. The honest answer is the reason to keep reading.
